other_observers: John Vanderpoel (who took video of the bird), Rich Levad; The bird was first found by John Vanderpoel and Peter Gent at Prince Res. #2 in Boulder Co. on the 9th of April and John put the definitive name to the bird on the 18th.
description: An immature gull somewhat similar to nearby First Basic Thayer's Gull, but much paler and without the darker primaries of that taxon. The bill was quite short and mostly black, but with a pale base, mostly on the mandible. The wings extended well past the tail tip with the tip of P7 falling just short of the tail. The primaries on the folded wing were fringed very widely with white, but because of the paleness of the primaries, the wide fringes made the primaries look white; they were very wide (much wider than found on Thayer's Gulls of the same age).  The tertials were white with a few brown bars and without a solid dark center. The legs were short and bright pink. The tail was darker than the rump, which was the palest part of the upperparts, being nearly white. The underparts were a creamy white with a variety of small darker splotches.Behaviors: Foraging in landfillCall: none heardPlumage: First Basic
